source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Vestibular and visual motion signals are well clustered in local spatial domains in posterior Sylvian area
Microstimulation with low currents reliably biased macaques' heading judgments particularly when based on vestibular cues
The bias direction was largely ipsilateral, rather than being consistent with prediction from the stimulated neurons' tuning preference
This microstimulation effect is in sharp contrast to the tuning-dependent effect as seen in other sensory systems
The hemispheric-readout algorithm may be related to the strong lateralized information processing originating from the peripheral vestibular system
This distinct readout mechanism predominates over the labeled-line code in the central vestibular system

typical_pattern: Opening statement of main finding or question
Comparison with previous studies or alternative explanations

likely_priorities: Causal evidence linking neural activity to perception
Comparison with established labeled-line coding principles
Spatial specificity of effects across brain areas
likely_attacks: Divergence between choice probability and microstimulation effects
Absence of microstimulation effect in PIVC despite its necessity shown by inactivation
Limited understanding of how peripheral push-pull organization is preserved in central pathways
Possible missed key subregions in PIVC due to its large anatomical extent
Weak clustering of vestibular signals in PIVC limiting microstimulation efficiency
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly discusses the divergence between CP and microstimulation effects, stating 'we also found a divergence between the two measurements'. It addresses the surprising absence of microstimulation effect in PIVC by listing 'a couple of possibilities' including that 'necessity and sufficiency are two different, complementary causal roles', 'poor clustered vestibular signals in PIVC may have limited microstimulation efficiency', and 'Our microstimulation in PIVC instead, may have missed these key subregions'. The paper also acknowledges that 'how the peripheral push-pull organization is preserved or transformed as signals ascend through the central vestibular pathway remains poorly understood'.

examples.quoted_phrase: microstimulation with low currents reliably biased macaques' heading judgments particularly when based on vestibular cues
We infer such a distinct readout mechanism observed in the neocortex may be related to the strong lateralized information processing
Our findings reveal a hemispheric-readout algorithm that predominates over the labeled-line code
it is likely that the vestibular signals in MSTd may not be critical for instantaneous heading judgments
which may constrain the way of information integration across sensory modalities

phrase_evidence.phrase: In the current study, we investigated causal roles of vestibular and visual motion signals
providing a basis for applying microstimulation to artificially manipulate neural activity with high spatial and temporal precision
This microstimulation effect is in sharp contrast to the tuning-dependent effect as seen in other sensory systems
There are a number of vestibular pathways in the brain mediating several fundamental as well as high cognitive functions
their exact functions are largely elusive, due to lack of studies involving neural activity recording, manipulation, and behavioral task at the same time
However, the debate about CP is that it is a correlation metric per se that does not necessarily indicate causality
we also found a divergence between the two measurements
This result makes it surprising in terms of the absence of microstimulation effect in PIVC in the current study
There are a couple of possibilities
necessity and sufficiency are two different, complementary causal roles that may not have to be yoked
the poor clustered vestibular signals in PIVC may have limited microstimulation efficiency in this area
Our microstimulation in PIVC instead, may have missed these key subregions
Compared to chemical inactivation, the electrical currents can be more precisely confined to local spatial domains
Here we show, however, microstimulation in the posterior sylvian area mainly induces ipsiversive heading perception
We infer such a distinct readout mechanism observed in the neocortex may be related to
a push–pull mechanism that smartly enhances signal-to-noise ratio of neural response to self-motion
how the peripheral push–pull organization is preserved or transformed as signals ascend through the central vestibular pathway remains poorly understood
This central spatial-temporal convergence of the vestibular signals from both hemispheres, is quite unlike the other sensory systems
Our findings reveal a hemispheric-readout algorithm that predominates over the labeled-line code
which may constrain the way of information integration across sensory modalities
Identifying functional links between neural activity and behavioral perception is a fundamental neuroscience issue
Using perturbation methods, numerous studies have provided direct evidence showing that sensory information in many systems is causally read out
electrical microstimulation in VPS significantly biased macaques' heading judgments toward the ipsilateral hemisphere
irrespective of the artificially-stimulated neurons' encoded heading preference
This effect was observed exclusively in the vestibular but not visual condition
Schematic of the virtual reality system
Timeline of heading discrimination task based on vestibular or visual heading stimuli
Anatomical location of VPS
MRI reconstruction of microstimulation sites

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided abstract, discussion, and figure legends.
